Threat.value: I react with alarm when someone touches me unexpectedly
Sudden movements by others put me immediately on guard
I feel on guard even inside my own home

Memory.value: I feel like I am only remembering parts of what happened and the rest is missing
Sometimes I feel the full emotional intensity of the event without any clear image or memory
My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.

Negative Appraisals.value: My body keeps reacting as if I am still in danger even when I am safe
I can't rely on other people

vignette:
Michael is a 56-year-old social worker who has remained in a relationship but has become increasingly tense and exhausted by his symptoms. His difficulties began after a natural disaster struck the area where he was living and working. He describes the moment of peak danger as the sudden roar of the event, the air filled with a burnt smell, and the shock of not knowing what was coming next. He remembers being on edge while trying to keep himself and others safe, and noticing how quickly ordinary sounds became alarming afterward. Motorcycle noise outside his building and sharp gunshot-like bangs from television or the street now hit him with the same force as the disaster, and the smell of something burnt is especially hard to shake.

When Michael talks about the event, he does not give a smooth story. He jumps between fragments and fixed scenes: a clear detail of the noise, a flash of smoke or heat, and then gaps where he says parts are simply missing. At other moments he speaks about the full terror in his body without being able to place it in a complete picture, as if the feeling arrives whole before the memory does. He can also describe some aspects of the disaster in unusually sharp detail, which makes the unfinished parts stand out even more.

Michael’s central belief is that his body is still betraying him and that other people are not dependable. In his words, “My body keeps acting like I’m still in danger even when I’m safe,” and “I can’t rely on people; they can’t be trusted.” He speaks with a bitter certainty that the world remains unsafe and that he has to assume others may not be there when it matters.

At home, Michael stays on guard even inside his own house. He flinches when his partner touches him unexpectedly, and sudden movements in the kitchen or hallway make him tense immediately. At work as a social worker, he notices himself scanning every corridor and doorway, especially in buildings where motorcycles pass outside or where a burnt smell from nearby cooking or faulty wiring drifts in. He becomes watchful for threat in ordinary sounds, and he is often mentally braced before he even knows what he is reacting to.

The cycle is self-reinforcing. Triggers such as motorcycle noise, gunshot-like sounds, and burnt smells pull up the disaster and intensify his sense of danger. That threat state then sharpens his fragmented recall and reinforces the idea that his body is unsafe and other people cannot be trusted. Those appraisals keep him tense and vigilant, which in turn leaves him even more likely to notice and react to the same cues. In his relationship, this shows up when he pulls away after being startled by a touch, leaving his partner confused and Michael more convinced that closeness is not safe.
